text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 16 April 2019 

Dr Priya Menon
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Priya Menon
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Carter, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: K732154 NHS No. 9076314528  
Flat 12, 28 Harcourt Road, London, SE15 2LG

Thank you for asking me to review this gentleman, a warehouse picker working between tall racking and narrow aisles where lifting tasks and pace vary by shift. He attended with his partner and provided a clear history. He has a diagnosis of focal epilepsy, with prior auras of a rising epigastric sensation and brief behavioural arrest occasionally progressing to impaired awareness. Workplace factors such as missed breaks, dehydration on longer shifts, and fluctuating start times have previously been noted by him to lower his threshold.

He withdrew from Clobazam on 13-Jan. At that time, he had 3 - 4 seizures, one precipitated by illness. He has remained stable without seizures since. He reports no nocturnal events, injuries, or prolonged post-ictal confusion in this seizure-free interval. He has continued levetiracetam and lamotrigine at unchanged doses and is adhering to these reliably. He denies adverse effects beyond mild fatigue late on extended shifts, which he attributes to workload rather than medication.

On examination today he was alert and oriented with normal speech and cranial nerve function. Motor and sensory examinations were unremarkable. There were no focal neurological deficits. Cardiovascular and general examination were normal. He understands SUDEP information previously provided and has a clear plan for rescue and workplace escalation if events recur.

Given his focal epilepsy and current stability following cessation of Clobazam, I have not altered his baseline antiseizure regimen. We discussed safety in the warehouse setting: he will avoid ladder work and unsupervised tasks at height, ensure regular hydration breaks, and continue buddy-working in narrow aisles as per occupational health recommendations. He is aware to report any breakthrough events promptly, particularly if associated with intercurrent illness or sleep loss during early or late shifts.

Plan:
- Continue lamotrigine and levetiracetam at current doses; no restart of Clobazam given stability since 13-Jan.  
- Occupational health liaison note provided (patient to share) confirming current seizure-free status since mid-January and advising continuation of existing risk mitigations.  
- Bloods today for routine monitoring (FBC, U&Es, LFTs) given ongoing antiseizure therapy.  
- If any recurrence of focal aware auras or impaired-awareness seizures, we will arrange ambulatory EEG and consider dose optimisation of lamotrigine.  
- Follow-up in epilepsy clinic in 4 months, sooner if seizures recur or work circumstances change.

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Clinic date is 16 April 2019. The letter states he stopped Clobazam on 13-Jan and around that time he had 3–4 seizures, then has had none since. From 13-Jan to 16-Apr is approximately 3 months. Because seizure-free duration is under 6 months, we should not label as seizure free and instead express a frequency over the recent period. Using the most recent ~3-month interval anchored by these dates, the count given is 3–4 seizures, giving 3 to 4 per 3 month.
